In accordance with the class-based structure of society accepted in Hinduism. This may be a "low" or a "noble" caste. The purest mercury, called "Brahmanical mercury," is considered the most potent substance for attaining an immortal body.

A common misconception is that the entire Bhagavad Gita is supposedly retold in the final chapter. Without reading all the chapters, some proceed directly to the 18th, which is fundamentally wrong. It is impossible to reach supreme knowledge by a shortcut.

…One day, a distant relative visited him. Still unwilling to believe that his nephew had become a Guru of yoga, he was blunt: "And what have you achieved in yoga?" Without hesitation, he replied: "Not much…" The uncle exclaimed with satisfaction: "Ha! I knew it!" – and that was the end of the conversation. What more could be said? It is impossible to express the experience and realizations of an entire lifetime in a couple of sentences. Impatient readers who have not mastered the preceding chapters of the Bhagavad Gita will never understand the concluding chapter that summarizes them. Without the painstaking processing of mercury, without admiration and reverence for the mysteries of Nature, a person cannot withstand the properties of the final product of Alchemy – pure Brahmanical mercury.

The refinement of nuances in acquired knowledge and the process of its "digestion" are equally applicable to both mind and matter. To approach the divine state, the sadhaka must pass through all stages of Kriya Yoga with the same perseverance with which siddha-yogis subjected mercury to various stages of transformation. Having absorbed all that is necessary and discarded the superfluous, the processed mercury acts as a catalyst for eternal life. A life that can be dedicated to the ultimate knowledge of Creation and the Creator, His (or Her) roadmaps and blueprints. Knowledge is infinite, and in the process of learning, one can create one's own worlds and realities.

**Khechari, the Key to the Inner Heavens**

Of course, to outsiders this mudra seemed more than strange. The tongue had to be "curled back" and inserted into the throat. Guided behind the upper soft palate, it had to remain in this position throughout the entire practice, for several hours. For the uninitiated, it is a bizarre and strange trick, yet Khechari is a key condition for progress in higher yoga.

…Having first read about it, he persistently sought those who knew and practiced Khechari and could teach him. It was the criterion of steadfast sadhana and the key to yoga. All subsequent stages of Kriya Yoga were given to students only after mastering Khechari; it had to be mastered before the more complex phases. Considered mystical even among practicing yogis, this mudra was almost forbidden, and little was known about its properties, and few practiced it. Initially disheartened by its difficulty, he mastered this mudra after a year and a half.

He usually demonstrated it to new students during initiation. Some had heard of Khechari before; the most ambitious would begin intensive practice and usually master it after some time. It was also believed that karmic predisposition played a major role in the successful performance of Khechari, but how is this strange position of the tongue connected to karma? Why did this particular organ play a decisive role in continuing the yogic path? Certain hints were contained in old books, and some answers he discovered during his personal sadhana.

Interestingly, the name for the tongue, "Jihva," in Sanskrit also means one of the names of Shakti. It is believed that Shiva cannot exist without Shakti, and Her help is necessary to attain the state of Shiva. Shakti is power, desire, hunger, the thirst to attain, absorb, and assimilate. The satisfaction of desires has many forms, and Khechari Shakti is the most refined of all existing pleasures. In Kriya Yoga, Khechari mudra is the "bridge" between the lower centers and the "entrance door" of the soul, the Brahmarandhra, or fontanel at the crown of the head. Perfected, this mudra connects the lower and upper centers, physically and metaphysically. "Bram" means "mind," but this word can also be interpreted as a connection with Brahma the Creator.

The tongue acts as a receiving device, delivering amrita to the heart of the yogi. During the practice of kriyas, the brain secretes certain hormones, and Khechari prevents the precious drops of nectar from being burned in the digestive fire.

The tongue is important: it governs speech and communication, expresses thoughts, and perceives taste. What would happen if this organ did not exist? In yoga, the tongue is a magical instrument, literally leading to the transformation of the soul. The encoded meaning of Khechari was secretly transmitted by ancient masters to those who wished to practice true yoga.

Those who could not perform Khechari would eventually abandon the practice, finding it strange and difficult, and the mudra something unattainable. "Karmic predisposition" was mentioned above. True, there existed rather extravagant methods of achieving the mudra by cutting the thin connective membrane under the tongue. It is believed that the desired result can be achieved this way, but in the tradition of Lahiri Mahasaya, it is not encouraged.

"Cutting" the lingual frenulum and forcibly stretching the tongue can irreversibly damage the most delicate nerves in the tissues of the organ; the tongue must remain untouched. The factor of slowing down aging is an indicator of positive progress in this yoga. Conversely, rapid physical decline indicates that something is going wrong, a possible serious damage to the nervous system. He knew two people who underwent "circumcision" to achieve Khechari, and both aged with frightening speed, despite their practice of Kriya Yoga. Furthermore, the procedure is technically complex and was used only as a last resort.

In 37 years, he himself cut students' tongues perhaps seven or eight times, in cases where they could not progress in practice after many years of steadfast sadhana. Sometimes certain anatomical features prevented the tongue from stretching, and only cutting gave people a chance to move to the next stage of kriya… All procedures were successful, although no one had ever taught him this. Somehow he instinctively knew where to make the incision and how deep. Two or three microscopic drops of blood would appear, and a slight swelling might develop the next day. A tiny incision only 2 millimeters long, but this was usually enough to perform the mudra. However, all this was useless without special tongue-stretching exercises, practiced for a long time before the procedure. Without them, the tongue could not "stay" in place.

All yogic texts agreed on the uniqueness of Khechari; it is Shiva's favorite mudra, bestowing protection and the power of clairvoyance. Everything written about Khechari in the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* is true. Although there was a subtlety: results came if all ten mudras mentioned in the text were successfully mastered. Then the yogi, drinking amrita with the help of this mudra, could avoid aging and death. The tongue "milked" the brain, which yielded the precious nectar, and only by becoming a magician could one obtain the magical elixir of eternal life. Performing the seemingly impossible condition, Khechari, seemed quite fair…

One secret yogic text said: "He who attains perfection in Khechari attains the state of 'Khecharatva'," – "moving in the sky." He never met a person capable of flying with the help of Khechari, and assumed this statement was more of a metaphorical comparison than a description of a process. The *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* described Khechari as a state of mind that has entered the Void. Everyone who attained Khechari moved in the heavens of their own mind.

Another treatise, the *Gorakshasataka*, summarized: "The mind moves in the ether (khe), because the tongue moves in the void (khe); this is the Khechari mudra, worshipped by all siddhas."

As a result of persistent practice, the yogi could achieve union with the consciousness of the Void. The expanded consciousness of the yogi absorbed higher knowledge and brought him into a completely new reality. Physical limitations lost their significance; the yogi transcended the body. From a practical standpoint, the mudra provided powerful protection from any possible physical harm, which was confirmed by a number of cases, including those he personally experienced.

Alchemical texts noted Khechari as an important condition for processing mercury. The liquid metal had to be processed exclusively while holding the Khechari mudra and a specific mantra, and the presence of people with physical deformities during this process was forbidden. Why? The texts gave no explanation, but emphasized this condition as mandatory. Alchemical manuscripts also recommended wearing necklaces made from human bones or the teeth of a black donkey, combined with reciting certain incantations during the "work" on mercury. However, in his experiments, he used nothing but Khechari. The slightest error in reciting the mantras could spoil everything, and any mistake could be fatal.

In those days, he lived in a cottage; there was plenty of space for alchemical experiments, and there was no shortage of volunteers. For nine months, he and his students continuously processed mercury with various herbs, preparing it for the final steam bath. The mercury had to be boiled in 40 kg of mustard oil until the liquid completely evaporated, and the substance was already at the 6th stage of processing. A fire was lit under a huge cauldron; all four participants in the experiment stood around: himself, his friend Ram Babu, and two other students. Each held their tongue in the Khechari mudra. The liquid was half evaporated when they noticed an elderly man running towards them. Convinced they were working on obtaining alchemical gold, Vyasa-ji, who had arrived unannounced, wanted to join the ritual.

He was missing one finger and, in addition, had some kind of growth on his head. According to the texts, physical imperfections made him absolutely unsuitable for participation in the process of alchemical mercury processing. At that time, none of them knew anything about the specific requirements of the alchemical process, and besides, there was no time to stop Vyasa-ji. An explosion occurred: the cauldron, the cylinder, and all the ingredients were destroyed; the remains of the mustard oil intensified the fire. The pale flame of the explosion was visible from afar; bushes and trees within a radius of 7 meters were burned to the ground, but the participants miraculously survived, as if the explosion passed through them without causing harm. The Khechari mudra had saved them.

The Guru's duty also included warning those on the path of higher yoga about the necessity of strictly following all instructions. The Teacher's words about the meticulousness of performing techniques are by no means general recommendations; they are a guarantee of survival. The same rules applied to Ayurveda and Tantra, where success depended on the precise dosage, correct preparation and administration of the remedy, combined with the right intentions. Yes, the main focus of his interests was yoga, but Alchemy and Ayurveda aided in sadhana. With the help of correct alchemical ingredients and effective tantric principles, yoga-sadhana yielded tremendous results.

All these disciplines carried a vast amount of Vidya, Knowledge, gleaned by him from the volumes of the Universal Encyclopedia. Spirit was the main element of Life, and its distillation and refinement is the most intense process. The secret alchemical element of yoga, Khechari, could transform a seeker into a siddha, one who reaps the fruits of achievement, exploring the heavens of the Self.

**Sister**

* * *

“…True relationships are not of blood, but of the heart. Like lovers or spouses: people not bound by blood ties can become examples of the strongest bond.”
— Devahuti Pathak

Devahuti

Apart from other factors, the presence of a brother and sister at the *smashan* ensured the success of summoning the Avadhut to the cremation ground (or to any other place) – this is precisely what Ganganath communicated during their memorable meeting. Nath said literally the following: "For the Avadhut to come, a brother and sister must live on the *smashan*." This was as sudden as if someone had unexpectedly shone a flashlight in his face. There was ample food for thought, and he did not then dare to interrupt his interlocutor to clarify this statement. Attempts to find confirmation in legends and myths yielded no results. It seemed this secret was known only within narrow yogic circles, and knowledge of the specific aspects of summoning the Avadhut was a privilege of the chosen few. The sacred energy of Shiva and Shakti in its pure, sattvic form – the secret ingredient, the key to the success of invoking the Deity.

And yet, in the fact that the "little sister," as he called Devahuti, was one of the key participants in this phantasmagoria, there was nothing surprising.

The ninth daughter and twelfth child in the family, she possessed hidden abilities that manifested themselves under the most unexpected circumstances. It seemed her presence was the key to many inexplicable phenomena, a condition for many events. Her prophetic dreams foretold large-scale changes, mystical premonitions were reinforced by the intuition of the heart, and it was she who found their future home, the *smashan*. Various Gods and astral beings came into her dreams, sending messages and blessings. She was destined to be by his side to maintain the fragile balance of hidden forces, to ground them; through her, Devi Braj herself watched, breathed, softened sorrows and illnesses. Perhaps all these assumptions are speculative, and yet, intertwining with subtle magic, life itself compelled the acceptance of this theory as a given. Under the lens of the *smashan*, their destinies were seen quite clearly; both were confident in the rightness of their chosen path. Yoga was his destiny, and her lot was service to the Teacher, the Guru, and the brother.

Short and laughter-loving, she was the strongest woman he had ever met. Like his mother, but softer, more "domestic." The women of their clan belonged to the warrior caste, were persistent, often stubborn, with a heightened sense of justice. Devahuti's character was cut from the same cloth as his own; yet he always knew: beneath the thin steel armor she donned in the world, she was soft-hearted and compassionate.

From early childhood, she was impossible to ignore; without any pressure, she displayed persistence and inquisitiveness, taking an interest in life and people. Wherever she found herself, her presence was felt by everyone and in everything. He was grateful to her for many things: for the palace she found; for the food prepared without any outside help, with which she managed to feed endless crowds of guests; for her sharp mind and jokes, which always lifted his spirits; for periodically shielding him from intrusive visitors. Having taken upon herself the task of maintaining his *sadhana* at any cost, she selflessly fulfilled this unspoken obligation – so that he could remain on the path of yoga, without burdening himself with worldly concerns.

Devahuti was responsible for his immaculate clothing: snow-white in summer, black in winter; the *kurtas* were ironed, folded, washed, and they continued to materialize daily. The vast household required attention and constant upkeep: a large house, kitchen, cow sheds, farm chores, sowing, harvesting, and fodder supplies. She was a born manager, skillfully managing all of this, including him, as he liked to joke.

…The first meeting with his future companion, friend, and confidante was unremarkable. He was nine, and they were living in Shivpuri when an aunt visited them with her daughter, seven or eight months old. He was the youngest among his brothers and sisters, and the appearance of a tiny cousin gave him a chance to become an older brother, protector, and mentor. For the first time, he felt responsibility and cared for a small, vulnerable being completely dependent on his guardianship. She followed him everywhere she could crawl on her own. A strong bond formed between them, and when his family moved to Gwalior, the aunt and Devahuti visited them quite often. Her name meant "call of the Gods" – and she could summon anything she wished, even as a child.

She was in middle school when she mistakenly drank poison from her father's home laboratory – and died a completely real death. Like many Indian doctors, her father experimented with various remedies, and their little pharmacy was cluttered with jars of ointments, bottles of syrups, and various boxes of pills. Doctors' children, as is known, are savvy about health matters, and Devahuti was well-versed in symptoms and medicines. That day, suffering from a severe cough, she swallowed a syrup from a familiar bottle, only fleetingly surprised by the strange taste. It turned out to be chloroform. She lost consciousness right in the living room, during a family dinner. At the hospital, her stomach was pumped urgently, but despite all measures taken, her pulse weakened and soon disappeared entirely, followed by her breathing… The weary doctor who came out of the operating room told the relatives that she was gone. However, about an hour later, she suddenly began to breathe, to the astonishment of the medical staff and the relief of the shocked family. Having fully recovered, she told an astonishing story.

…Sitting in that same parental home, she was looking out the window: a spaceship resembling a giant marigold flower was landing on their lawn, and she ran out of the house. The flying vehicle had seven seats, and six ancient sages, *rishis*, were already seated there. The seventh seat was intended for her. One of the *rishis*, with a white beard and kind eyes, asked her very gently: "Child, do you wish to come with us?" At that moment, she heard her mother crying and replied that she would like to stay with her mother for a while longer, to comfort her. The sage said: "Stay with her. When the time comes, we will return for you." She woke up disoriented, with a bitter taste in her mouth. The astral adventure lasted only a few minutes, but more than an hour had passed since her breathing stopped.

…As they grew older, they remained friends, still feeling at ease in each other's company. The path he had chosen, the stories he told, and the plans he shared, she considered righteous, truthful, and perfect.

The shock caused by the death of his elder brother Mahendra, and the almost demonstrative disregard of some relatives for their family's grief, made him abandon any career plans. The naked, blatant meaninglessness of life filled him with heavy sadness, and at times cold anger – and Devahuti, even as a child, sensed his state of mind, although the age difference prevented her from understanding the nuances of the changes taking place within him. And yet she tried to encourage him, to help him confront grief, difficult situations, and painful conflicts.

In 1990, she asked for initiation into Kriya, and after initiation began calling him Guruji, like the other students and visitors. In the evenings, when everyone had left, she would sit down at a low coffee table and talk about the day's events. Her voice was high and clear, like a bell, and her mood was most often quite cheerful. Sitting in the huge white house on the *smashan*, in inhospitable and unfamiliar lands, they laughed and joked, although the situation was not conducive to merriment. The chatter and anecdotes they exchanged, the songs from Bollywood films that they both loved and sang, cemented their friendship, which had begun long ago, on the day a chubby little girl began persistently following a unsmiling boy with deep, dark eyes the color of *jamun* berries.

Having moved to the *smashan*, she poured all her energy and love into that land, into the people who became more her family than hired workers. But her most sincere friends and admirers were the animals, and she was always surrounded by a devoted company of dogs, bulls, antelopes, and monkeys.

At the foot of the sacred Hill, she founded a just and prosperous kingdom. Those *rishis* from the spaceship could wait; she had too much unfinished business here. She arranged marriages and prepared lavish festive meals, dealt with financial troubles and quarrels among the workers, sang songs, and healed everyone with the firm hand of a hereditary medic. And she nursed him back to health when he fell seriously ill.

**The *Sadhana* of Life**

She was born into a most humble family, and her unremarkable youth suggested no deviation from the expected course of life. Like most girls of Braj, she was supposed to marry and start a family; the society of the North Indian province demanded nothing more from young women. But the strength of her will and devotion to her brother-Guru led them both to the most unexpected place.

In 1989, when Devahuti's elder sisters were married off, talk began of her proposed betrothal and a search for a suitable groom. But she flatly refused any further matchmaking proposals. Initiation into Kriya Yoga changed her attitude towards life, and she declared that she would follow the yogic path. The same one her cousin followed.

He warned her: "Think carefully. This path is not strewn with stardust and rose petals. In the countryside, even on the holy land of Krishna, most people are by no means filled with lofty thoughts and have poisonous tongues. A young man living under the same roof as an unmarried sister, even if he is a yogi, is an excellent target for the worst gossip and criticism." Devahuti replied that gossip did not trouble her in the least; she only wanted to follow him and support him in every way. Thus, the unspoken pact was sealed with mutual readiness to go through life on their own terms, regardless of society's expectations or censure.

When it became clear that Devahuti's choice was final, he said: "If you marry, you will have a husband and perhaps several children; but, setting out with me, you will become a spiritual mother to many. People will honor you for entirely different achievements, and you will live an unusual life, rarely granted to householders." And she chose that very life. Her mother believed in them both and supported their plan, while the other family members simply did not dare to object.

The beginning was not easy; overcoming numerous difficulties, Devahuti always took the initiative into her own hands. A new, unfamiliar place, an initially hostile attitude towards newcomers… Life in enchanted palaces was not at all like a fairy tale: there was an abundance of hard labor and sweat, funds were perpetually insufficient, and prospects for quick improvement or stability were absent. And yet, she showed not a shadow of regret or doubt, always finding ways to bring healthy, practical common sense into this new, strange life. And she did not at all mourn the predictability of the modest but secure future she had renounced for a life on the ancient *smashan*, amidst magnificent but crumbling palaces.

Her practice was unwavering, and her faith in the Guru was unshakable.  
He was her work, her responsibility, her family, and her Teacher.  
Once, she spoke of this once and for all in a casual conversation: "Perhaps I will not succeed in yoga, but I will do everything in my power to support Guruji's sadhana."  
And so it was.  

The hardships of the early years in Govardhan revealed her hidden abilities: it turned out she could command snakes, and they did not harm her. The area was teeming with enormous cobras; several times, while overseeing the work, she accidentally stepped on them. The snake would merely spread its hood, staring intently at Devahuti, and then slither away. Cobras, as is known, do not tolerate such treatment and usually bite their offenders, yet Devahuti remained unharmed. Then it became clear that she could command them, simply saying: "Begone!" – and the reptile would glide away. Sometimes, for amusement, she would order the cobra to return: "Come here!" The long, undulating gray-brown body would freeze, and after some hesitation, the snake would turn back.  

In one old photograph, Devahuti stands before the palace in a semi-trance, her eyes slightly upturned, her face serious and serene. A large snake is coiled around her neck, while several snake charmers in the background observe her with genuine respect… For her, all living beings were, above all… alive.  

Hospitable, open, fond of a sharp joke, Devahuti loved people, and they returned the feeling. Her unerring intuition usually allowed her to diagnose illnesses, and the knowledge she had gained from her father, a doctor, helped her heal them. But when he fell ill, her clairvoyance was as powerless as the doctors they consulted. He was losing weight rapidly, and she could not stop the inexorable decline.  

**The Mantra of Change**  

In the summer of 2007, he turned fifty, and a grand celebration was organized in Gwalior: loud, rather exhausting, but obligatory. So many people sought to express their respect, love, and admiration for him that it would have been impolite to deprive them of that chance. Even spiritual leaders (and they especially) cannot avoid certain social obligations. After the celebration, his body began to change, and these changes were so dramatic that friends and visitors became convinced he was seriously ill. From the sharp weight loss, his face became gaunt, his cheeks sunken, though he moved with his former confidence. With each day, the signs of physical ailment became more and more evident.  

The corset of muscles weakened; his lower ribs seemed to have collapsed inward into his ribcage. The changes were strange, inexplicable. All manner of specialists could not determine the cause of the malaise, as all tests came back normal. Some doctors suspected prolonged arsenic poisoning, as other hypotheses were refuted by test results. In general, there were grounds for such conjectures.  

The cradle of enlightenment, India, was in many ways no different from any other land. Nurtured by the worst qualities of human nature, ill will, intrigue, conspiracy, and envy flourish everywhere. In villages and cities, black magic is still widely practiced, and even blood ties are not a restraining factor. For the worldly person, there is no greater pleasure than to witness the suffering and death of a more successful, prosperous, or popular neighbor. Hypothetically, he did not fall into any of these categories, but he preferred not to share his thoughts regarding his own illness. Although he had theories about the nature of the malaise.  

After some time, he became so weak that Devahuti had to practically carry him on her back, helping him climb the stairs. The most difficult trial for him was not even the loss of muscle and increasing weakness, but a heavy, debilitating depression. And yet, certain yogic postures and mahamudras helped him keep from sliding into the abyss of despair and darkness. These exercises were known to be effective in cases of poisoning, and he struggled painfully and persistently with his body, forcing himself to complete the series.  

He was also tormented by a constant thirst, which suddenly passed a year later, in September 2008, when several Russian disciples made a pilgrimage to Mount Kailash. Having completed the ritual circumambulation of the holy mountain, they left his photograph near a small chorten, at the highest altitude accessible for climbing – as a sign of gratitude to the Teacher, who had opened new depths for them in the knowledge of the eternal, primordial Dharma. To the Teacher, who was their friend and who might now need any help at all.  

More than anything, this strange illness resembled a planned transformation, rather than the result of black magic, and he associated it with the mysterious mantra he had received on January 10, 2005.  

…After the Department of Forestry transferred the land adjacent to the shamshan to the management of the Siddha Siddhanta Academy, he began expanding the plantation and earthworks. Bulldozers were leveling a kilometer-long stretch of the future road; the days were full and long. There was little time, or rather, no time at all for anything but work. They slept little, in snatches. On the night after the roadwork was completed, Devahuti had a dream. Around three in the morning, when he had just begun to fall asleep after his night practice, she knocked on the door of his room, and then began shaking him by the shoulders: "Wake up, wake up! It was Parashurama!"  

In her dream, the great immortal was emerging from the shamshan pond after bathing. Standing on the steps, he was wringing water from the long locks of his jata; the falling drops were heavy and viscous, like honey. Devahuti saw herself handing him a folded cloth, something like a towel. The Avatar's gaze was fixed upon her; his eyes were black and devoid of any expression, yet his voice sounded soft. As is usual in dreams, mental perception grasped both the meaning of what was said and the subtext. "Tell Shailendra that the time for his initiation has come." The dream was significant, as Parashurama was the spiritual Teacher of their clan.  

Of course, he remembered the story of Parashurama's summons, told by the old Aghori many years ago, and since then he had learned much about the great warrior-ascetic, but he had never imagined that he would become part of his own story. Whenever this initiation was meant to happen, he was ready.  

Several months passed. The trees planted along the road laid out beside the Hill had taken root, and he was satisfied with the results. One day, after the practice was over, when the house had grown quiet and the night cicadas had finally put away their bows, he had a vision. In it, Devahuti was persistently leading him along the new road towards the plantation. The approaching dawn was slowly adding halftones to the palette of night; a gentle veil of mist enveloped the hill, and the melting darkness clung to its folds. Along the road on the slope stood several enormous gorilla-like ghosts, about 10 meters high, with huge, clawed hands folded across their chests. Silent, dark-skinned giants stared directly at them, but somehow it was understood: they posed no immediate threat. Following Devahuti, he approached a large stone in the shape of a cobra with its hood spread. A fire was burning before the stone.  

Straight out of the hillside, a young ascetic came towards them, wearing a white loincloth, no older than sixteen, carrying a staff and a kamandalu – a ritual water vessel made of coconut. Slender and tall, with very long, curly hair reaching his thighs, the youth radiated beauty and peace. The ascetic stepped towards them directly through the fire, and the bright flames tinged his skin a golden color. Then he felt Devahuti's hand on his shoulder; pushing him forward towards the fire, she stepped back. The youth gestured for him to sit, and he obeyed. Then the yogi (whoever he was) repeated an unfamiliar mantra three times, and each syllable was imprinted on his memory. The ascetic said: "Esco japa karo" — "continue repeating this japa." The words were in Sanskrit and had a deep meaning, yet the mantra was not connected with any tradition or deity known to him.  

The vision dissipated; coming out of the trance, he repeated the words until they became a part of him. Attempts to discover the origin of the mantra yielded no results. One thing was clear: this was a Guru-mantra, since anyone who transmits a mantra is a Guru – and this yogi with the golden skin was his astral Teacher. What he had received was called a "Swapna mantra" – a formula received in the state between sleep and waking. It became part of his sadhana.  

Years later, in his commentary on the ancient text "Gorakh Both" (The Enlightenment of Gorakh), he wrote: "Most teachings are given on the physical plane, but some advanced seekers can receive an astral form of initiation. A yogi who has reached a certain level in his sadhana can receive initiation from an astral teacher: he gives him a special mantra without revealing himself. The teacher who initiated the seeker into sadhana remains his guru until the appearance of the astral master. He comes to help the worthy practitioner advance further. Sometimes the physical and astral guru are one and the same. Ideally, a teacher present on the earthly plane should be able to lead the disciple into the astral and guide him there, as well as provide the disciple with protection in the astral world. After many years, the true identity of the astral teacher is revealed, and this signifies the realization of the Guru."  

He was describing his own experience. The identity of the astral Guru remained undisclosed, but he believed: to those who know the value of waiting, everything necessary eventually comes.  

…He and Devahuti belonged to the Bhargava gotra, to the lineage of Parashurama – perhaps that is why the Guru of their clan (kula) came to bless his initiation in person. In any case, the physical and mental changes began from the very day he started reciting the new mantra.  

By January 2008, his weight had dropped from 100 to 62 kg. Devahuti, who was far more worried about him than he was himself, insisted on consulting ever more specialists in search of a diagnosis and treatment. The doctors performed complex tests, but were unable to make a definitive diagnosis. Only his blood sugar level was above normal; otherwise, there were no abnormalities. Apart from the pain in his legs and the weight loss, he himself could not identify any other anomalies. He perceived the pain as a sign of the transformation that accompanies any serious change, but he did not cease his sadhana, no matter how ill or depressed he felt. At times he understood: if he had known in advance that the mantra would bring about such dramatic shifts on the physical plane, he would have feared such an effect.  

During these events, which could be called nothing other than a trial, he did not attempt to establish mental contact with the teachers, knowing that Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, Babaji, and the parampara were watching over him. And in a difficult moment, they would take care of him, removing obstacles, sending relief, as had happened before. He had already undergone serious trials several times, and was now confident that his life was not in danger.  

The very fact that Babaji had told him to begin a new practice on the day of his 57th birthday gave him courage. The Mahavatar had foreseen everything, and would hardly have taught him if he were not meant to survive. In moments of extreme weakness and depression, when hope for improvement was fading, he perceived this as a promise for the future.  

The mantra received on the astral plane affected the physical body in a certain way – and the body simply had to cope. Such was his assumption, and his intuition usually did not fail him.

**Departure**

Devahuti learned of her illness in the spring of 2013. Instead of following the doctors' insistent advice and agreeing to chemotherapy in a hospital, she decided to stay home and meet her fate. The night when her Spirit departed, leaving the motionless body on

Only the chipmunks and birds, usually fed on the patch of ground before the kitchen, scurried about with their former energy, but even their chirping and twittering seemed muffled.

This brief astral meeting gave him hope for a new beginning, whatever it might be. **The Return of Shakti**

consecration, they speculated about the life plan destined for Yashashwini.

I wept so much
that my tears dried up,
abandoning me.

only they
