Gurukula

* * *

“It is said that the children of the *gurukula* (the teacher’s house) are of five kinds: the children of *ayi*, the children of *vileshwara*, the children of *vibhuti*, the children of the *nathas*, and the children of *yogishwara*, and all of them possess different qualities.”
Fifth Instruction; *shloka* 43 of the *Siddha Siddhanta Paddhati*
Shri Gorakhnath

Children of the Mothers

None of the Teachers in the lineage of Babaji belonged to the Nath tradition. Kriya did not require the initiations prescribed for the Nathas, but traces of this teaching were evident. Those who could practice this yoga were called “children of the mothers,” “born of women.”

The ancient text *Siddha Siddhanta Paddhati* enumerates several types of students who were welcomed into the Guru’s house. They formed a family, a *gurukula*, where students received knowledge and sometimes performed household chores. This is part of the tradition, and he regarded it with respect. Visiting the Teacher’s house in Benares, he, on a par with other students, sometimes helped with the housework, considering it an honor. The Teacher lived alone, and such help was necessary; moreover, the students were part of the *gurukula*, his family. But years later, when his own household began to require unceasing support and daily maintenance, he preferred to hire workers. Students should only practice yoga.

All who came to him for knowledge were children of *Ayi*, children of the mothers. Ordinary people who had chosen an extraordinary path. The others mentioned in the ancient text were *siddhas*, created by the power of the Guru. *Vileshwara* were naked ascetics, *digambaras* or “sky-clad” Naga Babas, and the children of *vibhuti*, the Aghoris, Pashupatas, Nathas, were so called because they smeared themselves with ash.

He regarded those who came to him for guidance primarily as children of Mother Earth. Nature herself encouraged all human achievements, providing the components and equipment for any experiment and investigation, including yoga. Observing people, he rejoiced in their progress, the awakening of dormant abilities, their becoming what they were meant to be — knowers. The students of the Siddha Siddhanta Academy represented all strata of society: among them were doctors and teachers, farmers and merchants, jewelers and politicians, military personnel and engineers, scientists and police officers. They were Hindus, Chinese, Jews, Ukrainians, Italians, Finns, Brazilians, Russians, French, and Germans. What mattered was that they all aspired to yoga, and this desire stimulated a rethinking of their own identity.

The experiment of Kriya yoga, conducted by the immortals over centuries, is but one of many experiments on human society. The initiation of Lahiri Mahasaya lifted the veil of exclusivity and secrecy that had so long concealed the teaching. Of course, the practice itself was not revealed to the uninitiated; the transmission of knowledge was still carried out individually. Before the beginning of the spiritual journey, the Guru weighed and assessed each one’s potential, for not all were ready. The *parampara* is selective. But one thing remained certain: the *kriya* given by the immortal Teacher bore within it the seeds of immortality, and this definitely helped to prevent old age and even death. To a certain extent. If Babaji himself had attained the highest of yogic states, then the keys to immortality must be hidden in the techniques of His teaching.

Why did all these people want to become his spiritual children? Why, regardless of age, gender, and nationality, did a close, trusting friendship with the Guru eventually arise in each one? After all, he taught only breathing techniques; the rest of the time passed between endless cups of tea and varied discussions, exercises in the gym, and short but powerful nightly rituals.

The answer is simple: trust grew out of friendship, and thus a unique bond was established between Guru and disciple. It is impossible to feign a sincere interest in people’s destiny and spiritual development; the teacher must be genuinely interested in the person he is teaching. The student must possess potential, a spark of a certain adventurousness — and the mutual trust that develops over time is the most important ingredient in the alchemy of teaching. And then this currency inevitably turned into the gold of faith. Faith in the Teaching, in the Teacher, in the rightness of the path once chosen.

Probabilities and Predictions,
Motives and Expectations

What would have happened had he continued his military career? The result of such a choice remained in the gray zone of probabilities. Most likely, having followed the trajectory of an army career, he would have turned into a grumpy brigadier, or perhaps attained a higher rank. Probably, he would have spent his evenings in the officers’ club, cursing stupid civilians, sipping whiskey surrounded by shabby plastic chairs, unrealized opportunities, and similarly disillusioned colleagues.

According to the horoscope, even a military career would have been unsuccessful. Of course, Vedic astrology cannot predict the future with absolute accuracy; this ancient science contains unique information, but correct interpretation is crucial. Only a few can “transmit” information so that the inquirer can hear and understand. Not everyone pays attention to the warnings and recommendations of the stars; people want to hear only positive predictions. Since the planets indicate possibilities and tendencies, it is necessary to heed their hints. By the time he had become a Guru, many astrologers spoke of numerous signs pointing to the spiritual path destined for him, but all this had already happened. The past does not tolerate subjunctive moods — that is the prerogative of destiny. He had already begun the journey, and the “forecasts” turned out to be yesterday’s news. Only one royal astrologer from Gwalior, a man nearly a hundred years old who still retained a passion for his profession, cast his horoscope with high accuracy, and most of the predictions came true.

The horoscope was cast when he, as a homeless Guru, lived in an ashram in Vrindavan, when the palaces from visions were still dreams, a whisper of promise, fragments of a misty future. The old astrologer then said: “This young man will live in a huge ashram near Mathura” — and the prediction came true. The period of Rahu’s transit in his chart indicated an influx of visitors from distant lands: “Numerous foreigners, *angreze*, will study under him…” And this proved accurate, but in 1992 no one could have imagined such a development. The students who had asked the astrologer to read their Guru’s natal chart tried to clarify something, but he snorted: “Don’t get your hopes up, he won’t waste time on ignoramuses like you! He will be surrounded by millionaires!” But those days were still ahead.

Honestly, he never thought he would become a Guru; he simply wanted to practice to the best of his ability and attain realization, whatever that might imply. At that time, he did not particularly understand the role of a non-religious Guru, thinking that a Guru of Kriya yoga was someone who transmitted various techniques, like an instructor. Only later did he realize: this role is by no means exhausted by teaching methods and techniques, and the practice itself is but one of many steps on the path of spiritual development.

Like the late Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, he received visitors daily. The questions varied, and for correct guidance, concentration and clarity of consciousness were required. Realized consciousness. He was aware of the degree of responsibility for each person’s destiny. To guide and console those who sought advice or peace was akin to balancing on a tightrope stretched over a karmic abyss. The consequences of wrong advice could be extremely serious for both the teacher and the student.

At first, when the Teacher asked him to answer visitors’ questions during *darshans*, he felt constrained and tense. That he was proclaimed Guru of the *parampara* after two years of practice was a bewildering surprise, almost shocking. However, the uncertainty of the state soon gave way to confidence.

He learned to see the deep causes of each question; while the words were still balancing in the air, they were already forming into an answer. People dressed the skeleton of doubt in words, veiling vague, unformed desires with long phrases. Sometimes syllables hid deep remorse, and intentions glimmered within the phonemes. The more answers to questions he gave, the easier it became to see the nature of each question.

Shaniya and the sought solution. The invisible door opened with each answer, and through it poured a stream of truth. An answer intended for a specific person could resonate with the unspoken thoughts of others present, and standing in the midst of this field of knowledge, he gathered an astonishing harvest and shared it with everyone. This knowledge was a property of Prana; it was diffused in the very air that everyone breathed.

The collective portrait of the seekers was incredibly diverse and included both those who had never performed any asanas and experienced yoga instructors. He had nothing against the latter. By introducing people to a healthier lifestyle, familiarizing them with various ways of feeling bodily comfort, yoga teachers were doing a good deed. They helped people find peace of mind, to feel the forgotten joy of physical wholeness. Having achieved the highest results in the yoga of the body, some began to realize that beyond the asanas there was something more, and they began the search for a higher yoga.

Having heard the silent call, learning of it from friends or through word of mouth, they found the path to the smashan, to him. Experienced in the art of asanas, the teachers were highly disciplined and worked diligently. Their advantage lay not in superior physical fitness, but in a firm faith in discipline and perseverance, in the determination to develop new astral muscles and crack the code of enlightenment.

Problems appeared after a couple of years (and sometimes earlier), when some realized that the practice of Kriya is for life. And it was rather “practicing” them, and not the other way around. For some yoga instructors, the bonuses began to seem dubious – this Kriya could not be passed on to others and, consequently, it did not contribute to commercial success, was not a commodity, and took up too much time. Thinking and social connections, habits, aspirations, and priorities changed, and goals were reset to zero. Life seemed to be getting out of control, rather than being harmonized and balanced. Some were troubled by the lack of visible signs of progress, and they insisted on seeing the “promised” results, believing they had proven their loyalty to the practice long enough and were ready to enjoy its fruits.

Observing people over many years, he clearly saw: success in this yoga depended on motivation. There were people who resembled warm champagne: as soon as the bottle was opened, there was abundant foam, but what remained of the bubbling liquid barely covered the bottom. Overly enthusiastic and exalted representatives at the beginning would “deflate” after some time. Those who were truly passionate about yoga did not flaunt their feelings or make plans, but moved slowly towards their intended goal.

The motives that brought people varied. Some had not the slightest idea about the aspects of higher yoga and came along with friends. Others were intrigued by the personality of the Teacher, the mysticism of the practice and the place itself. And some needed an anesthetic, a painkiller for the torment of reality.

Some of the Indian disciples belonged to the third or fourth generation of Kriya Yoga practitioners. For them, it was a family tradition, not a personal choice. Initiated by Shri Satyacharan Lahiri himself, their grandparents were already advanced in years when he himself was declared the successor.

Only a few realized the ultimate goal from the very beginning. Remembering the years of his own search, doubts and fears of never finding a teacher, the indifference, misunderstanding, and skepticism of so-called well-wishers, he supported all practitioners. Not only in their striving for yoga, since he himself never considered that the sole purpose of life. As a rule, no one knew what the achievements would be. Beginners hoped to experience Samadhi, Nirvana, bliss, whatever that state was called, and in most cases had not the slightest idea what could happen to their bodies and psyche. As a rule, their expectations were radically different from the realities of the sought-after result. Again and again he described the side effects and aspects of states after samadhi, and the picture was far from glamorous.

Students spoke of intriguing things read in books: about the rise of Kundalini energy through the Sushumna channel, about piercing the chakras… He listened patiently and with interest, and at the end gently informed them that no one had chakras yet, they must first develop them. He taught them, as an artist teaches an apprentice: to stretch the canvas on the stretcher, to prime it, to carefully mix paints and make several sketches before beginning to paint an epic canvas.

But overall, he was proud of his disciples, their steadfastness, will, and unwavering self-knowledge. Persistent practice changed the contours of the future. Each had to discover the essence of their own Self, which wore such different masks. Like a coat rack completely hidden by clothes, the core of the personality was concealed under layers of masquerade costumes, and he taught people to get rid of unnecessary coverings and false ideas about their own nature.

**The Artist**

Name withheld, Bali, Indonesia

…Having first heard of him from his disciples, I simply listened, without trying to inquire about details. These people were serious practitioners, not schizoterics preaching homemade esoteric dogmas, and I treated them with respect. What they described sounded intriguing even to me – and in 20 years of living in India, I had seen many amazing things. In short, my acquaintances strongly advised me to visit this Teacher.

By that time, I had already left the ISKCON community, received tantric initiation, and was zealously performing tantric sadhana, yet a feeling of strange hunger was growing. No, I was not a yogi, I had always been a devotee, a bhakta, and that is what defined my spiritual essence. However, bhakti did not quench my hunger. Remember the four types of believers described in the Bhagavad Gita? Some are the afflicted, others seek benefit, others knowledge, and finally, there are the wise. Being a mixture of all four types, I sought above all true knowledge. In any case, I did not crave any exotic spiritual experiences, as I had already seen much, and yet understood too little. It was necessary to change the record. I craved clarity, not new pieces and glimpses of Truth; I needed to assemble Her puzzle. I know it sounds too general and idealistic – but many begin the next stage of knowledge in this way. Each person can digest exactly as much knowledge as they are allotted to assimilate, and I wanted to test my limitations and the strength of the material I was made of.

This Guru came into my dreams twice before I finally made it to Govardhan. In one of the dreams, he stood in a garden surrounded by disciples examining the palms of his hands and marveling at the absence of life lines on them. Trying to see his hands, I craned my neck, and the Guru suddenly addressed me: “You should learn to read the lines of your own destiny.” Stunned, I jumped up in bed, realizing that the time for a visit had come.

…The chaos of dusty streets and the noisy bustle of the city were left behind; the greenery of the trees behind the rusty gates was calming and almost refreshing, the air smelled of datura flowers, peacocks flashed among the trees. The place looked exactly as it did in my dream: majestic mausoleums, a pond with steps, and a dense garden with a huge white house surrounded by hibiscus bushes and fig trees. I even stopped being nervous. But only until I entered the house and saw him.

Later I learned that he always wears dark glasses so that people, especially newcomers, can feel more comfortable. And also because one old farmer died after looking at him for too long. Although that was probably local folklore. In any case, I felt relief not seeing his eyes behind those black mirrored sunglasses.

Describing him is rather difficult. Not because of a lack of distinguishing features (oh, his features are more than exceptional!), but because of the power he radiated. Tangible, heavy, almost frightening, it cut through the silence. I still did not soil my dhoti (although it almost happened later), but bowed in prostration and spoke in broken but quite understandable Hindi, explaining who I was and how I found him. He listened.

Tea was served; for me, the faces of the Indians and Europeans present were blurred, forming only a background. He asked a couple of questions in English, and I answered, desperately trying to explain everything at once: my path, my search, my motives, myself. But it all seemed unimportant, and he echoed this thought, saying somewhat absently in response to my incoherent tirades: “Dvaita, Advaita, Tantra – none of this is truly important. Only your personal experience at this moment in time matters. You are an artist, gifted with a special view of reality, and you must be intuitive. Tell me, what do you see and feel here and now…”. And he continued his conversation with another visitor, not waiting for my answer.

And then I saw… I can only give rough, fragmentary sketches, describing what I saw in general terms. No watercolor of words can convey the battle scene of Life, painted with broad strokes of Light. I found myself surrounded by clots of this condensed Light, although no one else present in the room seemed to notice it. Eight or nine fluid, constantly changing beings hovered behind the sofa on which the Guru sat. With many arms, woven from iridescent light, translucent, luminous, with white, almost neon outlines, these beings (or entities) watched those present. They knew everything and saw everyone, they gave some kind of energy or impulses that filled the breaches in the torn causal bodies, karana sharira, of people – and took something in return…

Listen, I am no novice to miracles, and I know about different points of assembly in our perception. And I understand perfectly that when entering altered states, our attention passes through different levels of perception. First, people see familiar images, things they can identify according to their level of consciousness… Being an artist and a practicing devotee of Lord Krishna, I assumed these beings were yoginis or dakinis, goddesses, shaktis – and then my mind began to deceive me. The subconscious whispered – these are not yoginis, but ancient beings of light, terrifying and wrathful.

Then the mind fell silent. And without the aid of any logic, I understood: these Shaktis were forms of Prana, the sacred forces of Life, guarding and guiding travelers. Of course, only those who knew the destination and had maps of the terrain. Walking these paths, one could enter different dimensions, if it was permitted. These were channels opened (or laid?) by ancient siddhas and sages, leading to different worlds. Using the channels of Prana and Light, mortals (certain mortals) could move to different floors of the Universe.

I tried to remain calm, knowing this was not a hallucination, I had been given a darshan, a gift of revelation, I had been allowed to see a portal to other worlds. There I saw pure Power and Knowledge that had fully known Themselves and had no need for a knower. In their presence, the witness disappears, itself becoming Knowledge. But, disappearing, you still remain. Nothing can take you away from your Self…

My interpretations of the vision ended, replaced by fear. We humans are always bound to form. Even our Gods are mostly depicted in human, or rather superhuman, but still recognizable forms. Form is called Nara, and it limits the human mind, since our perception presupposes form. I was horrified because I could no longer recognize forms, nor associate my concepts with them. I perceive reality in volumes – it is the artist’s curse… And I prayed, prayed to see the dear and recognizable form of the Lord, but

not this naked and merciless, annihilating truth  
of my own limitations, microscopic in infinite  
Time and Space. Who can bear  
the truth of Time?

Somehow pulled out of the body, I was shoved back  
into it. The entire trip took a few minutes, and the same force  
that squeezed me out like toothpaste from the tube of the body  
landed me again on the red carpet before the Guru. His form  
was certainly human, but I was no longer deluded  
about who he truly was. Oh no, he was not the Master  
of these powerful beings, these luminescent  
forms; they were here voluntarily. He was a conductor,  
catalyzing various forces – and these energies came to  
him because he is the rarest of mortals, able to see  
them, play with them, confront them…

Returned to the world of familiar concepts and sensations, I  
rejoiced in the cozy, habitual surroundings – just a room,  
I am just one of the visitors. Perhaps I sobbed and  
trembled – and even if so, no one reacted. The Guru did not  
ask whether I still wished to receive initiation into this  
yoga; he told me to go home, try to eat and get some  
sleep. And return the following week, if I wished.

I sat for a long time by the pond, unable to think or move.  
Several dogs, who had furiously barked at me at the gate upon my  
arrival, now surrounded me, licking my tear-wet face.  
I had not even realized I was crying.

It was a mistake to show me that radiant eternity; I would like to  
forget that terrifying sensation of separation from the body. Yes, I was  
shown absolute Freedom – from everything. But I myself was not  
free, and still wanted to remain in the body. Beyond absolute  
freedom lay absolute power, which must be controlled,  
having learned to interact with it. Despite  
what had happened, I decided to return.

Life beyond the walls of the *smashan* continued, loud and  
exultant, self-assured. I dragged myself to an auto-rickshaw,  
returned to Vrindavan, and slept for three days.

A week later, I came again to the *smashan*, and was told  
that the Guru would initiate me in a year. Giving me  
a set of exercises, he was straightforward: my body must  
be prepared for practice. Lord, how I sweated, doing push-ups…

You ask what I hoped to learn? Well, first  
of all, to know reality, to rid myself of bookish notions  
of Truth. And yes, I received what I asked for, at least  
a glimpse of understanding. This yoga is the art of surviving in the  
bright, merciless light of absolute reality, in which  
all shadows of personified thoughts vanish along with  
the logical mind. Absolute Knowledge contains the residual  
aspects of your personality. If you lack the strength to endure  
this light, it will burn everything in you, practically annihilating the  
sense of “I am.” Many think this is bliss,  
yet have no idea how terrifying it can  
be. This yoga is for the bravest. And, as in Tantra, purity  
is mandatory. *Sadachara*, or piety, is necessary  
more than anything else – so that the darkness that may crawl out  
of you does not build a nest in the heart.

I began to understand why the Guru speaks of immortal  
*siddhas*, bearers of the light of eternal Life, living in this  
merciless radiance of absolute Knowledge – they can  
play with their subtle and causal bodies, manifesting themselves at  
will, without leaving their forms. No, I do not think this is  
a fairy tale. India holds astonishing secrets, and  
knowledgeable people still live among us.

…You ask what exactly he teaches? Here is what I  
think: this Guru explains not only how to enter the light of  
*Samadhi*, but also how to survive that light. And to continue  
to exist in that state during life and after death.  
He possesses ancient knowledge of the light of Time.

A true devotee can say to God: “I do not care  
where I am, in hell or in heaven, but I will be with You for  
eternity, wherever You are…” This is *bhakti*, devotion – and  
this is what is my essence. I carry these words in my heart  
and intend to develop this resolve. I would like to remain in  
the Light of Time forever. Do I think of any benefits  
from this yoga? Yes, I would like to continue existing as  
a ray of pure consciousness. And in that state, I would like to continue  
creating.

The Astrologer

Michael N., Los Angeles, USA

He kept appearing during my nightly rituals,  
hovering in lotus posture above my modest home altar,  
in an astral but distinctly human form. Several weeks passed  
before I understood who it was. Since I am trained  
to ignore possible psychoses and hallucinations in  
practice, that is exactly what I did – I ignored him. In the end,  
having made out the rings on his fingers, I recognized him, as  
I had once seen a photograph. And yet I continued meditating  
until he said: “Are you going to pay me any attention at all?”  
Or something to that effect. When communication  
occurs on the subtle plane, the meaning is crystal clear, though the words  
may be anything. He made some adjustments to my  
practice. Those who practice yoga know of such cases  
of intervention by Teachers on the astral plane. Although  
technically he was not my Guru. Not yet.

After that, everything began to change. Practice quickly gained  
depth, though different from the yoga he taught  
students, as I later found out. Meanwhile, everything changed: my  
perception of Vedic astrology, my main sphere of interest  
and profession. Intuition sharpened. Even my  
level of intelligence noticeably increased, I dare say. New  
information was now “downloaded” directly, as inner  
knowledge, filtered through the heart. These positive  
changes came in leaps, becoming noticeable each time  
he appeared to me in meditations. He kept making  
changes to my *sadhana*. When I informed my then  
spiritual mentor about these events and changes, he  
said: “I see no problem. Read his books.  
You can meet him in person if you wish. We are friends;  
I will call him and introduce you, so you can visit  
him in India.”

At a retreat in Mexico, in Jalisco, after reading  
a few pages from his book “At the Right Hand of God,” I  
realized that this yoga would take me to higher levels.  
I had practiced *Kriya* for over ten years but felt I was not  
moving forward.

…At the beginning of the book, he compared the Earth to  
the Goddess Kali, and described earthly existence as a closed  
system, created solely for the knowledge of Death and Life.  
A giant laboratory, a school where souls receive an education.  
The study of souls is my profession, and I instantly  
understood what the author was talking about; I knew I had met  
the one I had sought all my life: a Master possessing a mighty,  
multidimensional, superhuman intellect. Never before  
had I read such honest and clear truth, sounding so  
simple. It is an epic symphony compared to the synthesized  
melodies of modern spiritual literature,  
which rather lulled than awakened.

Invited to a seven-day retreat in Puerto Vallarta  
as an astrologer, I did not intend to participate in the  
ceremonies. I simply did what I came for – cast  
horoscopes, read *Jyotish* for the seminar participants.  
I had plenty of free time, and one day,  
going out to the beach, I climbed to the top of a majestic  
cliff, resembling the prow of a ship sailing in the Pacific  
Ocean. There, meditating under the bright sun, surrounded by  
the exquisite blue of sky and water, I received the *darshan* of Guru  
Shailendra Sharma. It was quite shocking,  
since I had taken no mind-altering substances  
or potions, and it was not a hallucination or a “vision” – yet he was  
before me, on the subtle plane, and he said: “This time,  
try to experience the healing plants, immerse yourself in it.”  
So I did. With those plants and potions, everything  
I saw and felt, that entire mixture of darkness, false and evident  
pain, light, bliss, and liberation was powerful and vivid.  
I also learned much about people, observing them  
in the closed laboratory environment of the healing sessions. Later I  
realized that I myself needed to complete my school  
education before entering the university, before beginning the real  
practice…

I also had another fault: sometimes a righteous  
anger would boil up. Well, righteous from my point of view. An  
astrologer working with such ancient knowledge has no right  
to have distorted vision; he must be able  
to see through fire – and be that Fire. To serve for the Good,  
to serve what is right. And I saw, and what I saw  
was not particularly rosy. Working with people and for people,  
I felt growing irritation, since most often  
my clients did not understand that they were already blessed  
by the Spirit existing within them. The One who lived in their  
souls and hearts had already blessed them in this life – and they  
were blind and deaf. Therefore, while performing the duties of an  
astrologer, trying to explain to them how to use their own  
tools, I secretly resented it. Perhaps that is why I remained  
with my previous Teacher for almost eleven years – to  
work through pride, arrogance, and hidden anger. Another  
three years passed before I fully realized and  
heard the call of this Teacher, this *smashan*. Part of me  
regrets that my arrival was so long delayed, but if I had come  
immediately after seeing the Guru on the astral plane,  
I would still have been irritated and unbalanced…

At the end of February 2020, just before the start of the  
pandemic, I arrived in Govardhan after a stay in the Himalayas.  
I came with an indefinite, unspoken, hidden  
from myself desire to cease existence in the previous  
format. I was utterly exhausted by incorrect practice of  
*kriya*. The *sadhana* received from another Teacher, even  
after the astral corrections of Shailendra Sharma, was simply  
destroying me, despite all the amazing experiences,  
revelations, memories of past lives that had  
seeped into my subconscious over many years. My mind  
was very pliable, “porous,” and let through  
various beliefs, spiritual doctrines, and concepts. But I was  
emptied by the lack of vision of my own Path, the  
unclear prospects… No, I was not suicidal, but I wished to  
cease an existence full of pain and uncertainty; I cannot  
describe my mood then otherwise. In a sense,  
I was motivated only by the hope for the end of the previous  
phase; from meeting this teacher I expected a miraculous  
potion, an elixir of new life. And I received what I wished for, though  
at first I practically choked on the medicine prescribed to me.

Hearing the description of all the levels of this yogic  
practice and what was required for the “exit” from *samsara*, I was  
disheartened. The goal seemed difficult, unattainable, almost  
impossible. Honestly, thoughts of the probable futility  
of future efforts nearly crushed me. But the man  
who had passed through all these stages, days, months, and years of  
this *sadhana* and attained the goal sat right before me.

…On the second day of my stay, I realized I had finally  
arrived home. I usually sat at the far end of the hall, but  
obeying a gesture from the Guru, I moved closer and introduced  
myself, answering his questions: “Who are you? How old are you?”  
During the conversation, he said: “The Guru is your teacher,  
your friend, your parent, and your child.” I knew of the three  
aspects of the teacher in the Indian philosophical system, but had never before heard

so that the disciple perceives the Guru as his own child. He explained: the Teacher learns from his students – and the extraordinariness of this statement revealed itself to me gradually. The mutual, unceasing progress of the one who has attained heights and the one being taught was an illustration of the Unity of all in this world – Knowledge, the Knower, the Knowing.

Having been there, I understood Rumi’s poetry to its very core, his words about love, devotion, bhakti. Oh, how I realized with my entire being what the poet spoke of… I cannot convey the sensation of an explosion in the chest, the pain and bliss of a heart opening anew, and everything else I felt. I swear, there were moments when I thought I would simply die of love. Of this knowledge, of life, of the person who opened hearts. It would not be a bad way to leave this world, but before that, much work remains to be done.

How did he do it? How does he continue to do it with others? I have no idea. Most likely, his own Spirit establishes a connection with other souls deserving of such contact. And the healing begins… Why do you think he wears sunglasses during darshans? So that people do not burn in the gaze of love and compassion. That is my version, of course. He himself replies that he simply cannot bear bright light. To myself, I thought: “Well, of course, this is a smashan, a place where all old beliefs, perceptions, and illusions must die completely and irrevocably; this is how Time itself works.” Time is quite cruel in many respects. Although, that is precisely what I like about It, to be honest. I am a servant of Time, I work for It, and I have done so for many lifetimes. I will likely continue to serve It even after this current incarnation.

At the feet of this Teacher, one must be pure of heart. Intentions and motivation must be devoid of so-called “practical” justifications for choice. Having seen this there, I believed. Smashan is a special place where all worldly dross is removed, releasing the inner essence of a person; the cremation flame purifies the body after death, and yoga purifies it during life. In short, those who come to such places of their own free will know this.

So, if you intend to go there, to be in the presence of this Guru, you will have to leave all inner rubbish at the gate. This I understood very clearly. After a week spent at the smashan in his presence, my life changed forever.

Any Jiva, individual consciousness, must have something it can present to God. And I finally began to feel comfortable: through the art of reading the planets – not in a Vedic, but in a yogic key; I help those in need. Astrology develops an understanding of the futility of human existence, while at the same time giving birth to a deep respect for the many lives of the Soul. It passes through numerous incarnations, learning to discern, to discard or accept, to grieve and rejoice – endlessly, tirelessly, so that it may finally understand the absence of difference between all that exists, and know unity.

Meeting many people, I observed their nature, saw the roots of their present karmas, and try not to judge anyone, knowing how long each one’s path is… This is part of the work of oracles. After all, those like me are merely conduits of information. We exist to do work for others, not for our own gratification – and I am glad to help those in need. I like people full of hope; I myself am simply not like that. This is a very strange dharmic peculiarity of mine, which is present in my astrological chart… Not an absence of enthusiasm, but an absence of long-term hopes.

Jyotish is incorrectly translated as “the study of light”; it is not that. It is the study of electromagnetism against the backdrop of Time, betrothed to entropy, to decay. Entropy lives within Space, bound to the universal Order or Law, which organizes all causes and effects – those that have not yet arisen but already require ordering. United, Emptiness and the Order of Things give birth to Matter, which is in union with the conscious design of Creation, that very Absolute intelligence. Everything in the world is the final product of these forces – and therein lies the beauty of the world.

No, I did not read this in the Guru’s books; I have always known it. But his yogic commentaries, conversations with him, and his presence in my life finally put everything in its place. Today, the puzzle of existence has come together for me, albeit temporarily. Of course, I harbor no illusions that I have solved the mysteries of the universe. It is simply that this sadhana and this Teacher have finally charted a course for my self and mind, which had lived and learned so much that assembling all the experience had seemed impossible to them.

…The goal of immortality, as he explains it, and the formula for attaining eternal life are in many respects unattainable, inconceivable. But this is merely the muttering of a mind frightened by its own limitations, the commotion of the everyday personality that does not even try to know its own capacities. We dismiss things we cannot bring ourselves to attempt. But one yogi in a million (perhaps even that number is an exaggeration) goes all the way…

However, if you are able to find such a Teacher, receive his darshan, and follow him to walk the path that yogis who became immortal have walked, you have already stepped beyond time, matter, and space. Into another dimension of Time, into the entropy of eternity, where you will cease to exist in your former capacity, having become a vessel of Time. A bubble that refuses to burst.

Reading nearly the same words in verses written by the Guru himself many years ago, I felt my heart was healed. I was ready for the journey.

**The Second Coming**

Denis Z., Moscow, Russia

Everything that happened remains in the past, becoming merely a snapshot of memory (forgive the cliché). The memories of the search that changed my life resemble a travel journal I leaf through, recalling people, places, and teachings. Of course, today everything is seen in a different light than 18 years ago – and yet past events, goals, and actions proved to be fateful.

At that time, I was trying to assimilate the main currents of Hinduism and Vedanta, while simultaneously mastering the yoga of the body. All of this was meant to coalesce and reveal a single meaning, stripped of tinsel, pure and shining like a pearl freed from the folds of the mollusk’s flesh.

It was a kind of ambitious crusade. Tormented by spiritual hunger, I greedily devoured pieces of information and often suffered from indigestion. The hunger had to be satisfied, and I did not care about the taste of what I consumed. Greed was the main driving force; without it, I would have died of exhaustion. Therefore, I explored diverse paths and ran along several trails at once to reach a single goal – yoga. To definitively choose one tradition seemed simply impossible. The mind was impatient and uneducated; it had data but lacked a system of “record-keeping,” an organized file system. Of course, my methods were chaotic and immature, and yet I became a good yogi. There was no asana I had not mastered; my body knew everything, carried out the most severe commands, and endured everything I tried to manifest through it. However, I lacked concentration and simply could not cope with the numerous aspects of different teachings and traditions. Too many lines, interwoven, interconnected, hidden.

Having studied Hatha Yoga in the early nineties, I taught and practiced it. After the collapse of the Soviet Union, during the difficult times of perestroika, I even performed asanas on broken glass in the streets. Not out of great need, but to learn to concentrate in any environment, to demonstrate the body’s capabilities. And to give hope to others, if you will. Without these early stages, without hatha yoga, without the explorations, however naive they were, the continuation of the search in India, which led me to Kriya Yoga, would not have happened.

Even the most sincere seekers are deceived by their own hidden aims and motives. My goal was known to me, but what was the true motive? Perhaps the answer was hidden in the karmic history of the place where I was born – quite tragic, excessively permeated with violence and injustice. Too many restless souls dwell there. I felt spiritually vulnerable in Russia. Probably that is why the search for a spiritual Shambhala is so widespread among Russians, and many seekers believe in the existence of such a place. It exists, but it is an inner space, not a geographical point.

In January 2002, I received initiation into the Vira Shaiva lineage, and Kashi Jagadguru, Shri Chandrashekhar Shivacharya Mahaswamiji, became my first spiritual mentor. India exploded inside me like a time bomb. This country offered incredible opportunities for spiritual growth. And answers. I simply needed to ask the right questions. Over the next intense three months, I lived in the Jangamwadi Math in Varanasi and practiced. This Math, a center of Virashaivism, was one of the so-called pithas, sacred places; it contained everything needed: a temple, an ashram, a guesthouse, and the house where the Guru stays. Future swamis, jangams, live and study there for seven years, and then receive the degree of Shastri or Acharya at Benares Hindu University or Sampurnanand University.

The Math was rarely empty; rituals were performed continuously, pujas went on, crowds of pilgrims came and went – and for the first time I had the opportunity to gain an insight into traditional religious education from within. I studied Sanskrit and Hindi, sought additional information about teachers and living yogic lineages, but my main interest focused on the traditions of kundalini and Kriya Yoga.

Studying in Varanasi was certainly different from Western academic education. The intense pulse of the city’s ever-changing life, the bright colors, the narrow streets, the smell of smoke from burning pyres on the ghats by the river opened something in me, and I absorbed every particle of new information. Daily life would sometimes smooth the fabric of my mental and physical state, then crumple it again. Everything was permeated with the most diverse signals and signs; feelings and thoughts were complex, tangled. I gained access to certain knowledge. And for the first time, I realized its immensity.

Every day, our group of students met with the teacher, who spoke to us about Vedanta, Advaita, Brahman, and Atman. This man did not merely quote books; he practiced all of this, being a monk who had received knowledge through a comprehensive traditional education combined with

With practice. It was then that I understood I could never attain
the same level of knowledge. Never. And the realization of my own
limitations became a turning point. Standing before a mountain of
ancient knowledge, I understood: each discipline requires time,
one topic is connected to another. Conversing with scholars and
shastris, I realized that my notions of learning within the Hindu
tradition were strikingly different from reality, and that absorbing
this colossal body of material would take me an entire lifetime.

I belonged to another world; my cultural baggage did not
allow me to immerse myself in Hinduism as deeply as those who
were born here. I needed different codes, different keys, but there
were no shortcuts to Knowledge.

One of the professors, after observing me for some time, said:
“I have studied Sanskrit for almost sixty years, but much of what
is written in it I could not understand. Without a Guru, you will
not have complete understanding. To grasp not the linguistic, but
the spiritual aspect of the sacred texts, a teacher is needed.”

Yes, I definitely needed the help of someone who could give
form and continuation to the foundation of knowledge already
built, however carelessly it had been laid.

It is true that Varanasi creates a time and space in which one
can wish for anything. I intuitively felt: everything necessary was
within arm’s reach, and yet still at a distance. I wandered the
streets of the Old City, performed ablutions in the Ganga, did
pujas and recited mantras, visited temples, spoke with various
people—and still could not clearly formulate my request. Yet it
was heard.

For any yogi, personal presence in the sacred city of Shiva is
important; it is believed that a seeker can find a spiritual guide
only by the grace of the First Teacher of Yoga, Adi Guru Shiva.
One day, after standing in a long queue for the famous
Vishwanath Kashi temple, I entered the inner sanctum.
Approaching the Shivalingam, I heard a soft whisper of an inner
voice: “Just think of what matters most. Make a wish, and He will
fulfill it.” It was not a play of imagination, but a valuable and
timely counsel. At that moment, my head was overflowing with
fragments of thoughts, unfinished ideas, flashes of various
impulses, but I suppressed all of that and asked for a Teacher and
a teaching. And from that moment, certain existential forces,
which took control of subsequent events, seized me and carried
me forward.

…A couple of days after visiting the temple, one of the swamis
at the Math said he knew the person I needed. Without delay, we
got on his moped and went to the Benares Hindu University,
where he introduced me to K. M. Tripathi, the head of the
department of yoga. To him I told of my interest in yoga and
mentioned that I was seeking a Guru. Mr. Tripathi listened to my
disjointed story attentively and sympathetically, asking a few
questions which, in my opinion, were insignificant. And then, at
some point in the conversation, he showed me a photograph of his
own Teacher, the realized yogi Shailendra Sharma.

I had always believed that no photograph can reveal the
essence of a person; any image is deceptive. But for one who
needs a sign, an image can become a line of communication.
Looking at the photo, I felt something click inside me, and I
experienced a flow of strange, heavy energy—powerful and real.

If you are moving in the right direction, reality definitely begins
to change for the better. Over the following two weeks, I visited
K. M. Tripathi daily, asking questions and receiving answers.
Our sessions were purely academic in nature. I listened to lectures
on Kundalini yoga, hatha yoga, tantra, the history of existing
yogic lineages and practices, and all of this broadened my
horizons. The yoga I was seeking existed, but this teaching was
not given immediately to anyone who asked.

Mr. Tripathi advised me to go to this Guru in person. He had
already directed foreigners to Shailendra Sharma, but not one had
received initiation. There was no certainty that I could obtain
diksha. Nothing is ever guaranteed, but one must take risks. With
this thought, I set off for Govardhan, to see, to be seen, to ask,
and perhaps to receive what I so strongly desired—a map of the
route and a method by which I could meet myself.

The journey was far from comfortable. On the train, I was
severely poisoned; I vomited, I sweated terribly, and this brutal
cleansing continued for a long time. I do not remember how or
exactly when I reached Govardhan, how I entered the gates of the
smashan, or who led me to the Guru. Weak, exhausted, a
greenish color, I entered the house; but when I saw him,
everything became absolutely unimportant. All I felt at that
moment was relief: I had come to the right place.

Guruji received me warmly and even allowed me to stay in
one of the rooms of the palace. It was May; the daytime
temperature rose above 49°C; the electricity that could have
revived the small fan under the ceiling of the room was often out,
but I did not care. What troubled me was my own restless and
leaping mind. Intuition had not failed me; this man was my Guru.
But what next? Would he agree to accept me as a disciple?
Questions hung in the hot, sticky air. I stayed there for seven
days, rehearsing the key phrase: “Can I become your disciple?
Will you teach me?” When I finally asked this question, Guruji
said: “Guru is Guru, and I cannot initiate you into Kriya until you
obtain permission from your current Teacher.” Obviously, I had
to hit the road again. Besides, I needed to extend my visa.

A night train delivered me to Delhi; from there I headed to
Kashmir, where I hoped to resolve immigration matters with the
help of some dubious agency. This did not work, and I had to
leave Jammu. Reaching Lucknow on an overcrowded train, I
made another transfer.

…On the train to Gorakhpur, a kind family with children,
elderly grandmothers and grandfathers, offered me juice mixed
with strong sedatives, and I lost consciousness. Fortunately, my
passport was not taken, only some small change, along with the
money. They also cut off a small silver Shivalingam in a tiny
pouch that I wore on a cord around my neck. A pity. The drink
laced with the drug was so potent that I only came to in a hospital
in Gorakhpur, and the first person I saw was a policeman. The
Sanskrit textbooks found among my remaining belongings served
me well: the police assumed I was not a drug addict, but a
student. The police chief, himself a student of Sanskrit, took an
interest in me and came to talk. Then he helped with a train ticket
to Varanasi. All these signs indicated that any beginning can be
difficult, fortune is fickle, and I needed to focus better.

Arriving in Varanasi, I learned that my Jagadguru had
departed for Kudala-Sangama, one of the important centers of
Veerashaivism in the southern state of Karnataka. All five
Jagadgurus, about 15,000 sannyasis, and even more followers
gathered there annually. I did not have the patience to wait for the
Jagadguru’s return, and I set off again. The journey took about
three days: two train transfers and several hours by bus. Even in
2003, seeing a pale-faced European in South India was akin to
encountering an alien, and I was a rare curiosity. But the police
and locals were very friendly; no incidents or delays occurred.

…When I entered the hall, greeting my Jagadguru and
disregarding all protocols (not out of disrespect, but out of
ignorance), there were twenty high-ranking spiritual
officials—acharyas—in the hall. Of course, he was surprised to
see a crazy Russian with a backpack, soaked through, but with an
unquenchable flame in his red, sleepless eyes. I was given a place
to stay at the Math, and the next day we all set off for a recently
built ashram, in a small village not far from Bijapur. On the way
there, I asked the Guru if I could receive initiation into Kriya
yoga. He agreed, on the condition that I would keep the vows in
the practice he had given me. There was not a shadow of conflict
between these traditions, and time proved that the combination of
Kriya and Veerashaivism was strong and beneficial for me.

The railway saga continued. A few days later, I returned again
to Govardhan, and on June 6, 2003, Guruji Shri Shailendra
Sharma taught me the first level of kriya, and this became the
beginning of a transformation. Physical, mental, informational.
The practice opened a new stream of sensations and cleansed all
the mental sludge. I dared to enter the unknown space of the Self,
which I had never encountered before.

Being near Guruji, I was finally able to exhale after all the
travels and adventures. The integrity of perception and awareness
increased, and all of this happened solely due to the presence of
the Guru. Of course, the Teacher does not perform sadhana for
the disciple; each must achieve everything on their own, and I
worked diligently. Although I was not sure I could achieve the
same effects in practice outside the smashan, far from the
Teacher, far from India.

In Kriya, the last five parts of Patanjali’s yoga are realized:
pranayama, pratyahara, dharana, dhyana, and samadhi. The Guru
transmits not only techniques but also motivation. Even after
many years of sadhana, it is not always clear where exactly it will
lead. This discipline is a one-way ticket, and most people who
truly immerse themselves in it usually do not “return.” They live
and often succeed in worldly life, yet the focus of their interests
lies beyond the secular goals of society. I had always been an
outsider, so this aspect did not trouble me or create particular
difficulties.

Eighteen years have passed. Today, I feel an unbreakable
connection with both Teachers. And it seems I have received
everything I asked for; this is a blessing and an immense gift. I
understand the importance of the past, but I do not dwell on it. I
would like to be in Govardhan more often, near Guruji. There, at
the smashan, everything is saturated with completely special,
pure, and powerful creative energies. The Guru’s power and his
grace support all disciples, and yet we must learn to stand on our
own feet. To grow, to move forward, to develop emotional
independence and confidence in our own strength.

After many years of travel and learning, I am slowly
unpacking my enormous baggage, extracting more and more
various things from the metaphysical backpack. The meaning of
different techniques and darshans received, the power of holy
places, deities, and teachers slowly reveals itself to me. Spiritual
thirst made me drink from many sources, and today I am grateful
for the Knowledge gathered. Now, all acquired understanding
flows as one stream, feeding the lake of the mind. The wisdom
passed on to me together with blessings, and the grace I received,
came from a land that still calls to me. But I travel less now and
